questions.text: I pick up on when someone is narrating an event with a particular audience member in mind.
I notice when someone's politeness has an edge to it.
I experience joy with an intensity that sometimes surprises the people around me.
My emotional life has a consistent, predictable quality that I've come to rely on.
I think about the second- and third-order consequences of my decisions.
I rarely detect the difference between someone making small talk and someone trying to connect.
I feel that spending lavishly on my own experiences is a worthwhile use of money.
I enjoy being the one others look to for decisions.
I feel that the weight of suffering in my moral consciousness is the feature that makes my ethical outlook genuinely responsive to the conditions of real human lives.
After receiving bad news, I can feel the shock reverberating through my body for hours.
I think that the ability to process painful experiences through a rational framework is what makes it possible to provide sustained help without burning out.
I instinctively take control of high-pressure situations.
I find budgets and financial plans too restrictive and prefer to manage money intuitively.
I approach important decisions by gathering information and analyzing options systematically.
I find that volunteering my time for others gives me more fulfillment than personal leisure.
I believe that the pull to act on behalf of those who are suffering is among the most ethically sound motivations a person can follow.
When I have a vision, I wait to see if others share it before moving forward.
I feel that my primary function in relationships is to give, not to receive.
